fuzzy_prd:
We need a management layer in PHP that drives the system package manager to install, link, and control packages and their background services, so our local development tool can manage its own runtime dependencies through a clean interface without scattering raw shell strings everywhere. The layer should be able to check whether a named package is installed (by inspecting the metadata the package manager returns), determine whether any supported PHP version is currently installed from a fixed list of recognized version names (both the dotted style and the older compact style), register external package repositories before installing from them, start and stop background services (service restart requires a plain stop then a privileged stop then a privileged start; service stop requires plain stop then privileged stop), install a package with optional repositories and fail loudly with a normalized error if the manager exits non-zero, link and unlink package binaries with similar error handling, and detect which PHP version the package manager has linked by inspecting the symlink on disk. All shell commands issued must be traced in order in the output as 'cmd=...' lines so callers can verify the exact commands used. The invoking user name for user-context commands is a fixed value in the test environment. Errors must be machine-readable category codes, the same approach used by the PHP detection and service-control parts of the system.

One small follow-up after the team questions: the supported PHP names we should treat as valid are exactly 'php', 'php@5.6', 'php@7.0', 'php@7.1', 'php@7.2', 'php@7.3', 'php@7.4', 'php@8.0', 'php@8.1', plus the legacy compact names 'php56', 'php70', 'php71', 'php72', 'php73'. If the package manager reports anything else, like 'php@5.5' or 'php@5.4', that should come back as has_supported_php=false. For linked PHP detection, we’re reading the symlink target path, pulling the version number out of that path, and then only returning it if it matches that supported set, normalized to the canonical 'php@X.Y' form.

Also, on the command wrapping, user-context package-manager calls are always run as sudo -u "testuser" brew <cmd>, and that applies to tap, install, link, unlink, and list. The testuser value is fixed and deterministic in the test environment. Anything privileged should be sudo brew <cmd> without the -u flag. For service control specifically, the fire-and-forget calls stay as the raw brew command strings. Restart should issue exactly 3 supervisor commands in order after the install check: 'brew services stop <service>', then 'sudo brew services stop <service>', then 'sudo brew services start <service>'. Stop should issue exactly 2 in order: 'brew services stop <service>', then 'sudo brew services stop <service>'. And same as the rest of this layer, command tracing still needs to show the exact cmd=... lines in order, and failures should use the same error=<snake_case> style as the other operations.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: function installed(string $formula): bool; function hasSupportedPhp(): bool; function tap(array $formulas): void; function restartService(string $service): void; function stopService(string $service): void; function install(string $formula, array $taps = [], string ...$options): void; function link(string $formula, bool $force = false): string; function unlink(string $formula): string; function linkedPhp(): string

trigger_keywords: what error is reported when install fails
normalized error for failed brew install
install failure error category and fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: On install failure: two lines are emitted after the cmd line: 'error=install_failed' then 'formula=<package-name>'. On link failure: 'error=link_failed' then 'formula=<package-name>'. On unlink failure: 'error=unlink_failed' then 'formula=<package-name>'. On service check failure: 'error=services_check_failed'. On PHP not linked: 'error=php_not_linked'. On unsupported PHP version linked: 'error=unsupported_php_version'.

trigger_keywords: what command lists installed PHP packages
brew command to list PHP packages
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The has_supported_php check issues the command 'brew list --formula | grep php' on the user-context channel. The installed check issues 'brew info <formula> --json'. A package is installed when the JSON response's first entry has a non-empty 'installed' array. Empty output or output starting with 'Error: No' means not installed.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: link and unlink package binaries with similar error handling
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Both link and unlink run on the user-context channel with an error callback. On success they return the manager's raw stdout rendered as 'result=<text>'. The --force flag for link appends ' --force' to the brew link command.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under laravel__valet/golden/cli/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_installed_check.json.description: Determine whether a named package is currently installed by the system package manager.

feature2_supported_php_present.json.description: Check if any supported PHP version is installed from the fixed supported set.

feature3_tap_repositories.json.description: Register external repositories in order, each as sudo -u testuser brew tap.

cases.expected_output:
cmd=sudo -u "testuser" brew tap php@7.1
cmd=sudo -u "testuser" brew tap php@7.0
cmd=sudo -u "testuser" brew tap php@5.6

feature4_1_restart_service.json.description: Restart a service: install check, then plain stop, privileged stop, privileged start.

cases.expected_output:
cmd=brew info dnsmasq --json
cmd=brew services stop dnsmasq
cmd=sudo brew services stop dnsmasq
cmd=sudo brew services start dnsmasq

feature4_2_stop_service.json.description: Stop a service: install check, then plain stop, then privileged stop.

cases.expected_output:
cmd=brew info dnsmasq --json
cmd=brew services stop dnsmasq
cmd=sudo brew services stop dnsmasq

feature5_install_formula.json.description: Install a package; normalize failure to error=install_failed + formula=<name>.

feature6_link_formula.json.description: Link package binaries; --force appends the force flag.

cases.expected_output:
cmd=brew link aformula --force
result=Some output forced

feature7_unlink_formula.json.description: Unlink package binaries; failure yields error=unlink_failed.

feature8_1_has_linked_php.json.description: Report whether the package manager currently has PHP linked as a symlink.

feature8_2_linked_php_version.json.description: Determine which supported PHP version is linked; error if not linked or unsupported.
